title: FSS AI Vocabulary
description: Authoritative, FSS-contextualized definitions of AI terms for the Federal Statistical System. Built for an audience expert in federal statistics and new to AI: every record carries a plain-language translation and, where the corpus speaks, an FSS-specific note and false-friend flag. Definitions are sourced to NIST, statutes, and OMB guidance; where no authoritative definition was in hand, needs_source=true rather than a non-authoritative cite.
source_policy: Public, freely-accessible authority only. No paywalled sources (e.g. ISO/IEC standards behind a paywall are NOT used). Priority: US federal (NIST/OMB/statute) > open standards > open foreign-government or open industry/academic references > needs_source. A free, open definition always beats a gatekept one. source_quality is tagged honestly: where no US-federal definition exists, the record says so rather than dressing a foreign/industry source as federal.

definition.text: A machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments.

alt_definitions.text: Any artificial system that performs tasks under varying and unpredictable circumstances without significant human oversight, or that can learn from experience and improve performance when exposed to data sets; ... designed to think or act like a human ... or that is designed to act rationally.
alt_definitions.source: Sec. 238(g), John S. McCain NDAA for FY2019 (10 U.S.C. § 2358 note)

terms.plain_language: An umbrella term for software that does tasks we used to think needed a human — predicting, classifying, recommending, generating text — by finding patterns in data rather than following hand-written rules. There is no single legal definition; the statutory one (15 U.S.C. § 9401) is the federal anchor.
terms.fss_note: Covers any automated analytical system a statistical agency runs — imputation models, record linkage, automated coding, disclosure-avoidance engines. The label matters because OMB AI guidance (M-25-21) attaches obligations to 'AI' use.

fss_context.text: Covers any automated analytical system a statistical agency runs — imputation models, record linkage, automated coding, disclosure-avoidance engines. The label matters because OMB AI guidance (M-25-21) attaches obligations to 'AI' use.

definition.text: An engineered or machine-based system that can, for a given set of objectives, generate outputs such as predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy.

terms.plain_language: The whole working setup — the data pipeline, the model, and the output logic — not just the model file. When you evaluate AI risk, you evaluate the system, because harms come from how the pieces fit together, not the model alone.
terms.fss_note: Distinguishes the full deployed tool (e.g., a record-linkage system: input data + model + match-decision logic + output) from the model component. OMB risk-management practices apply to the system in use.

fss_context.text: Distinguishes the full deployed tool (e.g., a record-linkage system: input data + model + match-decision logic + output) from the model component. OMB risk-management practices apply to the system in use.

definition.text: A component of an AI system that implements AI technology and uses computational, statistical, or machine-learning techniques to produce outputs from a given set of inputs.
definition.source: OMB M-24-10 / NTIA AI Accountability Policy Report glossary

terms.plain_language: The trained mathematical object inside an AI system — the thing that turns inputs into outputs. Think of it like a fitted statistical model, but usually far larger and learned from data rather than specified by a methodologist.
terms.fss_note: In the FSS, 'AI models' include imputation models, disclosure-avoidance noise mechanisms, and predictive coding tools used in survey processing.

crosswalk.note: Both map inputs to outputs from data. Disanalogy: a statistical estimator has an explicit, documented functional form and known sampling properties; an AI model's form is learned and often opaque, so its error properties are empirical, not derived.

fss_context.text: In the FSS, 'AI models' include imputation models, disclosure-avoidance noise mechanisms, and predictive coding tools used in survey processing.

definition.text: The development and use of computer systems that adapt and learn from data with the goal of improving accuracy.

terms.plain_language: A way of building models by letting an algorithm find patterns in data and improve with more examples, instead of a person writing the rules. Most of what people call 'AI' today is machine learning.
terms.fss_note: Increasingly used in federal surveys for donor selection in imputation, nonresponse adjustment, and automated industry/occupation coding.

fss_context.text: Increasingly used in federal surveys for donor selection in imputation, nonresponse adjustment, and automated industry/occupation coding.

definition.text: The class of AI models that emulate the structure and characteristics of input data in order to generate derived synthetic content such as images, videos, audio, text, and other digital content.

terms.plain_language: AI that produces new content — text, images, code, synthetic data — that looks like its training data but is newly generated, not retrieved from a database. Large language models are the most familiar kind.
terms.fss_note: Introduces confabulation risk when drafting statistical narratives or metadata, and is an emerging tool for synthetic-data generation for privacy-protective dissemination.

fss_context.text: Introduces confabulation risk when drafting statistical narratives or metadata, and is an emerging tool for synthetic-data generation for privacy-protective dissemination.

definition.text: a class of language models that use deep-learning algorithms and are trained on extremely large textual datasets that can be multiple terabytes in size. LLMs can be classed into two types: generative or discriminatory.
definition.source: AI Assurance (2022), via NIST AI 100-3 (AIRC glossary)

terms.plain_language: A generative-AI model trained on very large amounts of text that predicts likely next words, letting it answer questions, summarize, translate, and draft. It is a fluent pattern-matcher over language, not a database of verified facts — which is why it can state wrong things confidently (see confabulation).
terms.fss_note: The tool most FSS staff will actually touch. Useful for drafting and summarizing; unsafe for producing statistics or facts without verification. NIST AI 600-1 (Generative AI Profile) is the governing risk reference — pull its exact definition before formal use.

fss_context.text: The tool most FSS staff will actually touch. Useful for drafting and summarizing; unsafe for producing statistics or facts without verification. NIST AI 600-1 (Generative AI Profile) is the governing risk reference — pull its exact definition before formal use.

definition.text: In generative AI, models trained on broad data using self-supervised learning that can be adapted such as through fine-tuning for a variety of down­stream tasks.

terms.plain_language: A very large model pre-trained on broad data that serves as a reusable base others adapt (fine-tune) for specific tasks. Most commercial LLMs are foundation models.
terms.fss_note: When an agency adopts a vendor foundation model, it inherits that model's training data, biases, and limitations — a value-chain risk that can collide with CIPSEA or SPD No. 15 category definitions.

fss_context.text: When an agency adopts a vendor foundation model, it inherits that model's training data, biases, and limitations — a value-chain risk that can collide with CIPSEA or SPD No. 15 category definitions.

definition.text: A dataset from which a model is learned.
definition.source: AI Fairness 360, via NIST AI 100-3 (AIRC glossary)

terms.plain_language: The examples a model learns from. The model's behavior, blind spots, and biases are inherited from this data — if a group is underrepresented in training data, the model tends to perform worse for that group.
terms.fss_note: Directly analogous to a sample frame's coverage: gaps in training data produce systematic errors the same way frame undercoverage does. FSS training data may come from administrative records or prior survey waves, each with CIPSEA and quality implications.

crosswalk.note: Both determine who is represented in what the system 'knows.' Disanalogy: a frame's coverage is measurable against a known target population; training-data coverage often has no enumerable target, so its gaps are harder to quantify.

fss_context.text: Directly analogous to a sample frame's coverage: gaps in training data produce systematic errors the same way frame undercoverage does. FSS training data may come from administrative records or prior survey waves, each with CIPSEA and quality implications.

(iv) include considerations for securing the training data, algorithms, and other components of any artificial intelligence system ag…
…earch, which often relies on access to the weights and training data of a model to perform scientifically rigorous experiments.

definition.text: The stage of ML in which a model is applied to a task. For example, a classifier model produces the classification of a test sample.

terms.plain_language: In AI, 'inference' means running a trained model to get an output for a new input — the production step, after training. It does NOT mean drawing conclusions about a population.
terms.fss_note: A hard FALSE FRIEND. To a statistician, 'inference' means reasoning from a sample to a population (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests). To an ML engineer, 'inference' is just 'the model made a prediction.' Same word, different concept — confirm which sense is meant in any AI procurement or methods discussion.

crosswalk.note: Statistical inference quantifies uncertainty about a population parameter from a sample. ML inference is the forward pass of a fitted model on new inputs and carries no population-level uncertainty claim by itself.

fss_context.text: A hard FALSE FRIEND. To a statistician, 'inference' means reasoning from a sample to a population (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests). To an ML engineer, 'inference' is just 'the model made a prediction.' Same word, different concept — confirm which sense is meant in any AI procurement or methods discussion.

definition.text: [concept drift] an online supervised learning scenario when the relation between the input data and the target variable changes over time.
definition.source: Gama et al. (concept drift), via NIST AI 100-3 (AIRC glossary)

alt_definitions.text: Systems that classify or predict a concept (e.g., credit ratings or computer intrusion monitors) over time can suffer performance loss when the concept they are tracking changes.
Use of a system outside the planned domain of application, and a common cause of performance gaps between laboratory settings and the real world.
alt_definitions.source: NIST SP 1270, via NIST AI 100-3 (AIRC glossary)

terms.plain_language: When a deployed model's accuracy decays over time because the world it sees no longer matches the world it was trained on (populations shift, definitions change, behavior changes). It degrades silently unless you monitor for it.
terms.fss_note: Closely analogous to a sampling frame going stale or mode effects shifting across survey cycles — the instrument slowly stops measuring what it used to.

crosswalk.note: Both are degradation of a fixed instrument as the target population moves. Disanalogy: frame obsolescence is auditable against updated frames; model drift needs explicit performance monitoring to detect.

fss_context.text: Closely analogous to a sampling frame going stale or mode effects shifting across survey cycles — the instrument slowly stops measuring what it used to.

definition.text: A process in which seed data are used to create artificial data that have some of the statistical characteristics of the seed data.

alt_definitions.text: a process in which seed data is used to create artificial data that has some of the statistical characteristics as the seed data

terms.plain_language: Artificially generated records that mimic the statistical properties of real data without being any real person's actual record. Used for privacy-protective sharing, testing, and model training.
terms.fss_note: An emerging disclosure-avoidance tool: synthetic microdata products let researchers work with statistically representative data while protecting Title 13 / CIPSEA confidentiality. Utility and disclosure risk must both be assessed.

fss_context.text: An emerging disclosure-avoidance tool: synthetic microdata products let researchers work with statistically representative data while protecting Title 13 / CIPSEA confidentiality. Utility and disclosure risk must both be assessed.

definition.text: A rigorous mathematical definition of disclosure that considers the risk that an individual's confidential data may be learned as a result of a mathematical analysis based on that data being made publicly available.

alt_definitions.text: A mathematical framework that quantifies privacy risk to individuals as a consequence of data collection and subsequent data release.

terms.plain_language: A mathematical guarantee that adding or removing any one person's data barely changes what is published, so an attacker cannot reliably tell whether you were in the dataset. The protection is tunable by a 'privacy-loss budget' (epsilon): more privacy means more noise and less accuracy.
terms.fss_note: The Census Bureau's Disclosure Avoidance System applied formal differential privacy to 2020 Census products — the first nationwide federal use. NIST publishes two official definitions (SP 800-188 and SP 800-226); both are kept here. Politically contested inside Commerce (see DAO-216-26).

crosswalk.note: DP is a formal, provable form of SDL with a quantified privacy-accuracy trade-off. Disanalogy: traditional SDL (cell suppression, swapping) gives heuristic protection with no formal bound; DP gives a provable bound at an explicit, debated accuracy cost.

fss_context.text: The Census Bureau's Disclosure Avoidance System applied formal differential privacy to 2020 Census products — the first nationwide federal use. NIST publishes two official definitions (SP 800-188 and SP 800-226); both are kept here. Politically contested inside Commerce (see DAO-216-26).

definition.text: General term for any process of removing the association between a set of identifying data and the data subject.

terms.plain_language: Stripping or masking the parts of a record that point to a specific person, so the data can be used without exposing identities. Note: de-identified is not the same as re-identification-proof — combinations of remaining fields can still single someone out.
terms.fss_note: The umbrella practice under which suppression, swapping, noise injection, and differential privacy all sit. CIPSEA's 'identifiable form' standard (44 U.S.C. § 3561) is the legal target de-identification must defeat.

fss_context.text: The umbrella practice under which suppression, swapping, noise injection, and differential privacy all sit. CIPSEA's 'identifiable form' standard (44 U.S.C. § 3561) is the legal target de-identification must defeat.

definition.text: ''identifiable form'' means any representation of information that permits the identity of the respondent to whom the information applies to be reasonably inferred by either direct or indirect means.

terms.plain_language: The reverse of de-identification: using leftover fields, outside data, or clever attacks to figure out who a 'de-identified' record actually is. FSS law defines the line in terms of whether identity can be 'reasonably inferred' by direct OR indirect means.
terms.fss_note: The legal harm CIPSEA confidentiality protections exist to prevent; the 'reasonably inferred by direct or indirect means' test is what disclosure-avoidance methods are measured against.

fss_context.text: The legal harm CIPSEA confidentiality protections exist to prevent; the 'reasonably inferred by direct or indirect means' test is what disclosure-avoidance methods are measured against.

definition.text: AI with an output that serves as a principal basis for decisions or actions that have a legal, material, binding, or significant effect on rights or safety.

terms.plain_language: The federal government's label for the AI uses that can actually hurt people or violate rights, which therefore get the strictest oversight. Under the current memo (M-25-21) this single 'high-impact' category replaced the older split of 'safety-impacting' vs 'rights-impacting' (M-24-10).
terms.fss_note: Determines which agency AI tools trigger minimum risk-management practices (pre-deployment testing, impact assessment, ongoing monitoring). Most core statistical-production AI is not high-impact, but tools informing individual determinations can be.

crosswalk.note: FALSE FRIEND: NIST's cybersecurity 'high-impact system' (FIPS 199) is about confidentiality/integrity/availability impact, NOT the OMB AI rights/safety sense. Don't conflate them.

fss_context.text: Determines which agency AI tools trigger minimum risk-management practices (pre-deployment testing, impact assessment, ongoing monitoring). Most core statistical-production AI is not high-impact, but tools informing individual determinations can be.

definition.text: An annual, public inventory of an agency's AI use cases, identifying high-impact use cases and describing associated risks and mitigations, required of federal agencies.

terms.plain_language: A yearly, public list every federal agency must publish of where it uses AI, flagging the high-impact uses and how it manages their risks. It is the main transparency mechanism for federal AI.
terms.fss_note: Statistical agencies must inventory production AI tools — imputation models, automated coding, disclosure-avoidance systems — beginning with the 2024 cycle.

fss_context.text: Statistical agencies must inventory production AI tools — imputation models, automated coding, disclosure-avoidance systems — beginning with the 2024 cycle.

definition.text: A senior agency official designated under OMB AI guidance to coordinate and oversee the agency's use of AI, including its risk management and the AI use case inventory.

terms.plain_language: The senior official each agency must name to own its AI strategy, governance, and risk. In statistical agencies this role overlaps with the Chief Data Officer and Statistical Official.
terms.fss_note: The CAIO's remit intersects the CDO and Statistical Official; in an FSS agency, AI-assisted collection and dissemination create shared responsibility across these roles.

fss_context.text: The CAIO's remit intersects the CDO and Statistical Official; in an FSS agency, AI-assisted collection and dissemination create shared responsibility across these roles.

definition.text: A structured, pre-deployment and ongoing evaluation of an AI use case's intended purpose, expected benefit, and potential risks, required as a minimum practice for high-impact (formerly safety-/rights-impacting) AI.

terms.plain_language: A required write-up, before you turn an AI tool on (and repeated periodically), of what it's for, who it could harm, and how you'll manage that. The AI equivalent of a methods-and-limitations review.
terms.fss_note: Principal statistical agencies must complete these for qualifying tools.

crosswalk.fss_concept: SPD No. 2 quality review / methodology review
crosswalk.note: Both are structured pre-release reviews of accuracy, fitness, and risk. Disanalogy: an AI impact assessment centers rights/safety harms and bias, which a classical quality review does not foreground.

fss_context.text: Principal statistical agencies must complete these for qualifying tools.

definition.text: Systematic and repeatable errors in an AI system that create unfair outcomes, such as privileging one group over another; arising from data, modeling choices, or human/societal factors (NIST distinguishes systemic, statistical/computational, and human-cognitive bias).
definition.source: NIST SP 1270 (Towards a Standard for Identifying and Managing Bias in AI)

terms.plain_language: When an AI system is systematically wrong in a way that disadvantages a particular group — often because the training data or design baked in an existing skew. It is not random error; it is patterned and tends to track demographic lines.
terms.fss_note: Central risk when AI is used for demographic estimation, record linkage, or automated classification; can deepen undercoverage of underrepresented groups.

crosswalk.note: Both are systematic (not random) distortions that hit specific subgroups. Disanalogy: nonresponse bias has an established estimation/adjustment theory; algorithmic bias often lacks a measurable true value to adjust toward.

fss_context.text: Central risk when AI is used for demographic estimation, record linkage, or automated classification; can deepen undercoverage of underrepresented groups.

definition.text: A group of people authorized and organized to emulate a potential adversary’s attack or exploitation capabilities against an enterprise’s security posture. The Red Team’s objective is to improve enterprise cybersecurity by demonstrating the impacts of successful attacks and by demonstrating what works for the defenders (i.e., the Blue Team) in an operational environment. Also known as Cyber Red Team.

terms.plain_language: Deliberately attacking your own AI system — feeding it adversarial or edge-case inputs — to find where it fails, produces biased or harmful output, or can be manipulated, before real-world use. AI red-teaming adapts the cybersecurity practice to model behavior, not just security.
terms.fss_note: Can stress-test FSS AI tools for demographic bias in classification, failure on edge-case data, or manipulation of public-facing NLP systems.

crosswalk.note: Both adversarially probe an instrument for failure modes before production. Disanalogy: cognitive testing studies human comprehension of questions; AI red-teaming studies model behavior under adversarial inputs.

fss_context.text: Can stress-test FSS AI tools for demographic bias in classification, failure on edge-case data, or manipulation of public-facing NLP systems.

definition.text: A property of AI systems concerning the degree to which the reasons for an output can be represented in terms meaningful to a given audience; one of the trustworthy-AI characteristics (explainable and interpretable).

terms.plain_language: Whether you can say, in terms a human can follow, WHY the AI produced a given output. A model can be accurate but unexplainable (a 'black box'), which is a problem when you must defend a methodology publicly.
terms.fss_note: FSS uses of AI for record linkage, imputation, or small-area estimation must be explainable to data users and oversight bodies, consistent with SPD No. 2 transparency expectations.

crosswalk.note: Both make a result auditable and reproducible to outsiders. Disanalogy: methodology docs describe a designed procedure; explainability must reconstruct the behavior of a learned model that has no hand-written procedure.

fss_context.text: FSS uses of AI for record linkage, imputation, or small-area estimation must be explainable to data users and oversight bodies, consistent with SPD No. 2 transparency expectations.

definition.text: AI that is valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair with harmful bias managed.

terms.plain_language: NIST's checklist of the seven properties an AI system needs to be worthy of trust. It is the evaluative baseline federal agencies use to judge whether an AI tool is acceptable.
terms.fss_note: The seven NIST characteristics form the assessment baseline for AI tools deployed by principal statistical agencies.

crosswalk.note: FALSE FRIEND: NIST's cybersecurity 'trustworthiness' is about security/reliability of an information system; 'trustworthy AI' is the seven-characteristic AI-RMF construct. Different frameworks.

fss_context.text: The seven NIST characteristics form the assessment baseline for AI tools deployed by principal statistical agencies.

definition.text: The production of confidently stated but erroneous or false content (also called 'hallucination' or 'fabrication') by a generative-AI system; identified by NIST as a pervasive generative-AI risk.

terms.plain_language: When a generative model states something false as if it were true — invented citations, wrong numbers, made-up facts — fluently and without warning. NIST prefers 'confabulation' over 'hallucination' because the model isn't perceiving anything; it is filling a gap with a plausible-sounding guess.
terms.fss_note: Critical risk when LLMs draft statistical reports, generate metadata, or summarize findings; mandates human verification before any figure or claim is relied on. FALSE FRIEND: an FSS reader may hear 'confabulation/hallucination' as harmless creativity; it is a specific risk of confident fabrication, and it is NOT measurement error (it can invent referents).

crosswalk.note: Both are content errors — a reported value departs from the truth. Disanalogy: measurement error is bounded around a true value and modeled as a distribution; confabulation invents referents that do not exist, so it has no true value to be bounded around.

fss_context.text: Critical risk when LLMs draft statistical reports, generate metadata, or summarize findings; mandates human verification before any figure or claim is relied on. FALSE FRIEND: an FSS reader may hear 'confabulation/hallucination' as harmless creativity; it is a specific risk of confident fabrication, and it is NOT measurement error (it can invent referents).

…larity, and reproducibility and to insulate from bias, fabrication, falsification, censorship, or other threats to integrity;

definition.text: The risk that AI-generated content undermines accurate, truthful, and reliable information — including disinformation, propaganda, or manipulative content; generative-AI risk category in NIST AI 600-1.

terms.plain_language: The danger that AI-made content pollutes the information environment with convincing but false or manipulative material. For a statistical agency, the worry is AI-drafted public text that misstates the data.
terms.fss_note: Applies when agencies use generative AI for press releases, data-product narratives, or public-facing summaries — official statistics carry an authority that misinformation can borrow.

fss_context.text: Applies when agencies use generative AI for press releases, data-product narratives, or public-facing summaries — official statistics carry an authority that misinformation can borrow.

definition.text: a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments.

alt_definitions.text: OMB M-24-10 uses 'automated decision-making systems' as an operative term — e.g. in its definition of automation bias: 'the propensity for humans to inordinately favor suggestions from automated decision-making systems and to ignore or fail to seek out contradictory information.'
any technology that either assists or replaces the judgement of human decision-makers.
alt_definitions.source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat, Directive on Automated Decision-Making (2021)

terms.plain_language: A computer process that makes a consequential decision about a person — or substantially drives a human's decision — with little or no meaningful human judgment (e.g. auto-approving or flagging a case). The concern is consequential decisions handed to software without meaningful human review.
terms.fss_note: No single U.S.-federal statutory definition of 'automated decision system' exists. The U.S.-federal anchors are the AI statutory definition (15 U.S.C. § 9401(3) — cited directly, NOT via the revoked EO 14110 that quotes it) and OMB AI-governance usage (M-24-10); the Canadian Treasury Board directive is the cleanest concise operational definition. Relevant in the FSS where data products feed individual-level determinations; pair with human-in-the-loop requirements.

fss_context.text: No single U.S.-federal statutory definition of 'automated decision system' exists. The U.S.-federal anchors are the AI statutory definition (15 U.S.C. § 9401(3) — cited directly, NOT via the revoked EO 14110 that quotes it) and OMB AI-governance usage (M-24-10); the Canadian Treasury Board directive is the cleanest concise operational definition. Relevant in the FSS where data products feed individual-level determinations; pair with human-in-the-loop requirements.

definition.text: The set of methods to reduce the risk of disclosing information on individuals, businesses or other organizations. Such methods are only related to the dissemination step and are usually based on restricting the amount of or modifying the data released.

terms.plain_language: The whole set of methods agencies apply before releasing statistics so that no individual respondent can be identified — suppression, rounding, swapping, noise injection, differential privacy. Required by the confidentiality statutes.
terms.fss_note: Required by Title 13, CIPSEA, and agency statutes. The Census Bureau's Disclosure Avoidance System (DAS) implemented differential privacy at scale for the 2020 Census. The choice of method is politically contested inside Commerce (see DAO-216-26).

crosswalk.note: Disclosure avoidance is the goal; DP, suppression, swapping, and noise injection are alternative methods that achieve it with different utility/privacy trade-offs.

fss_context.text: Required by Title 13, CIPSEA, and agency statutes. The Census Bureau's Disclosure Avoidance System (DAS) implemented differential privacy at scale for the 2020 Census. The choice of method is politically contested inside Commerce (see DAO-216-26).

definition.text: The set of methods to reduce the risk of disclosing information on individuals, businesses or other organizations. Such methods are only related to the dissemination step and are usually based on restricting the amount of or modifying the data released.

terms.plain_language: The technical name for the family of methods that reduce the risk that a published statistic reveals information about a specific individual or business. In the FSS this is the methodological core of disclosure avoidance.
terms.fss_note: The canonical FSS reference is FCSM Statistical Policy Working Paper 22, 'Report on Statistical Disclosure Limitation Methodology' (in this corpus as wp_22). Differential privacy is a formal member of this family.

crosswalk.note: Used nearly interchangeably; 'disclosure avoidance' is the Census Bureau's operational term, 'statistical disclosure limitation/control' the methodological literature's term.

fss_context.text: The canonical FSS reference is FCSM Statistical Policy Working Paper 22, 'Report on Statistical Disclosure Limitation Methodology' (in this corpus as wp_22). Differential privacy is a formal member of this family.

definition.text: Measurement error is the difference between the true value of the measurement and the value obtained during the measurement process.
definition.source: U.S. Census Bureau Statistical Quality Standards — Glossary

terms.plain_language: The gap between the answer recorded and the true value, caused by the respondent, interviewer, question wording, mode, or records. One of the components of nonsampling error.
terms.fss_note: A core FSS quality concept (U.S. Census Bureau Statistical Quality Standards; the D3 standard in this corpus is about producing measures of it). Its AI relevance: NLP tools parsing open-ended responses can introduce systematic measurement error that varies by respondent group, which is the bridge to AI confabulation/bias.

crosswalk.note: Both are content errors relative to a true value. Disanalogy: measurement error is bounded by a true value; confabulation can invent non-existent referents.

fss_context.text: A core FSS quality concept (U.S. Census Bureau Statistical Quality Standards; the D3 standard in this corpus is about producing measures of it). Its AI relevance: NLP tools parsing open-ended responses can introduce systematic measurement error that varies by respondent group, which is the bridge to AI confabulation/bias.

citations.snippet: Alternatively, the term 'measurement error' may refer to the variance (or standard deviation) of that difference.
…ve, sampling error, nonresponse error, coverage error, measurement error, and processing error, should be described and accounted for when reportin…
they may also be aimed at reducing measurement error be aimed at reducing measurement error

definition.text: Nonsampling errors are survey errors caused by factors other than sampling (e.g., nonsampling errors include errors in coverage, response errors, nonresponse errors, faulty questionnaires, interviewer recording errors, and processing errors).
definition.source: U.S. Census Bureau Statistical Quality Standards — Glossary

terms.plain_language: Every source of error in a statistic that is NOT from random sampling — coverage gaps, nonresponse, measurement error, processing mistakes. Often the dominant error in large surveys, and harder to quantify than sampling error.
terms.fss_note: The FSS error framework (Census Statistical Quality Standards; the D3 standard in this corpus) that AI systems must be evaluated against — AI can both introduce and mitigate nonsampling error, so it must be judged on all these dimensions, not just predictive accuracy.

crosswalk.note: Nonsampling error is the FSS's mature taxonomy of non-random error; AI bias, drift, and confabulation map onto its branches (coverage, temporal, measurement).

fss_context.text: The FSS error framework (Census Statistical Quality Standards; the D3 standard in this corpus) that AI systems must be evaluated against — AI can both introduce and mitigate nonsampling error, so it must be judged on all these dimensions, not just predictive accuracy.

definition.text: the term 'data asset' means a collection of data elements or data sets that may be grouped together.

terms.plain_language: In FSS law, simply a defined collection of data that can be grouped and managed as a unit. It is a governance/inventory term, not a technical or security term.
terms.fss_note: Carried by CIPSEA/Evidence Act §3502 and referenced across OMB memos (M-24-10, M-25-21, M-23-04); 80+ obligations in this corpus attach to 'data asset' (open-data, inventory, and Standard Application Process access duties).

crosswalk.note: FALSE FRIEND: NIST/CNSSI 4009 treats a 'data asset' as something to be secured (a cyber asset). The FSS statutory sense is a governed collection of data for inventory/access purposes. The obligations differ entirely.

fss_context.text: Carried by CIPSEA/Evidence Act §3502 and referenced across OMB memos (M-24-10, M-25-21, M-23-04); 80+ obligations in this corpus attach to 'data asset' (open-data, inventory, and Standard Application Process access duties).

definition.text: The term 'confidentiality' means a quality or condition accorded to information as an obligation not to disclose that information to an unauthorized party.

terms.plain_language: In FSS law, a binding duty NOT to disclose protected information to anyone unauthorized — a legal obligation, not just a security setting. CIPSEA makes willful unauthorized disclosure a federal crime.
terms.fss_note: The foundational FSS duty. Any AI use of statistical data inherits it: a model trained on confidential data cannot be deployed in a way that permits re-identification.

crosswalk.note: FALSE FRIEND: in cybersecurity 'confidentiality' is one leg of the CIA triad (a technical access-control property). In FSS law it is a statutory non-disclosure obligation with criminal penalties. Related spirit, very different force.

fss_context.text: The foundational FSS duty. Any AI use of statistical data inherits it: a model trained on confidential data cannot be deployed in a way that permits re-identification.

definition.text: Statistical products are the data and information (tables, estimates, microdata, analyses) disseminated by a statistical agency; the Trust Regulation frames duties of equitable access to and transparency about statistical products.
definition.source: Fundamental Responsibilities of RSAUs — 'Trust Regulation' (5 CFR Part 1321)

terms.plain_language: The published outputs of a statistical agency — the tables, estimates, and microdata it releases. FSS rules require they be produced with quality, released equitably, and documented transparently.
terms.fss_note: Governed by SPD No. 1/No. 4 and the 2024 Trust Regulation (5 CFR 1321); the disclosure-avoidance method applied to a statistical product is the live policy question in DAO-216-26.

fss_context.text: Governed by SPD No. 1/No. 4 and the 2024 Trust Regulation (5 CFR 1321); the disclosure-avoidance method applied to a statistical product is the live policy question in DAO-216-26.

definition.text: A de-identification technique that modifies a dataset by adding random values to the values of a selected attribute.

terms.plain_language: A disclosure-avoidance technique that protects respondents by adding small random values to data before release, so no single published number pins down one person. Differential privacy is a formal, calibrated way of doing this.
terms.fss_note: NIST defines it as a de-identification technique (SP 800-188). In the FSS it is one method of disclosure avoidance, closely tied to differential privacy. CONTESTED: DAO-216-26 (DOC, June 2026) bans 'noise injection' while OMB M-25-05 permits the technique with documentation — the conflict the KG surfaced (see docs/2026-06-22_lab_notes_m25_05_s317_closed.md). Stated neutrally; not adjudicated here.

crosswalk.note: Differential privacy is a formal, provably-calibrated form of noise injection; 'noise injection' also covers informal/heuristic noise addition without a formal privacy guarantee. M-25-05 footnote 89 names the two together.

fss_context.text: NIST defines it as a de-identification technique (SP 800-188). In the FSS it is one method of disclosure avoidance, closely tied to differential privacy. CONTESTED: DAO-216-26 (DOC, June 2026) bans 'noise injection' while OMB M-25-05 permits the technique with documentation — the conflict the KG surfaced (see docs/2026-06-22_lab_notes_m25_05_s317_closed.md). Stated neutrally; not adjudicated here.

definition.text: Colloquial term for confidently stated but false content generated by a model; NIST uses 'confabulation' (and 'fabrication') in preference to 'hallucination.'

terms.plain_language: The popular word for an AI confidently making something up. Prefer 'confabulation' in official writing — NIST does, because the model isn't perceiving anything, it's guessing.
terms.fss_note: Same risk as confabulation; the term you'll hear from vendors and the press. FALSE FRIEND: not a perceptual glitch and not measurement error — a confident fabrication. See [confabulation].

crosswalk.note: See confabulation: error vs. a true value, except confabulation/hallucination can invent referents with no true value.

fss_context.text: Same risk as confabulation; the term you'll hear from vendors and the press. FALSE FRIEND: not a perceptual glitch and not measurement error — a confident fabrication. See [confabulation].

definition.text: Bias is the difference between the expected value of an estimator and the actual population value.
definition.source: U.S. Census Bureau Statistical Quality Standards — Glossary (statistical sense)

terms.plain_language: A loaded word that means different things to different audiences. To a statistician, bias is a precise, often quantifiable concept: the expected difference between an estimator and the true parameter (and can be desirable, e.g., a bias-variance trade-off). In AI/ethics discourse, 'bias' usually means unfair, discriminatory outcomes.
terms.fss_note: A hard FALSE FRIEND — clarify which sense is meant. Statistical bias (estimator vs. parameter) is not the same as algorithmic/social bias (unfair group outcomes). NIST SP 1270 catalogs the AI senses; pull its wording for the AI definition.

crosswalk.note: Statistical bias is E[estimator] - true value, a neutral quantifiable quantity. AI/algorithmic bias is normative — patterned unfair outcomes for groups. Same word, different field, different valence.

fss_context.text: A hard FALSE FRIEND — clarify which sense is meant. Statistical bias (estimator vs. parameter) is not the same as algorithmic/social bias (unfair group outcomes). NIST SP 1270 catalogs the AI senses; pull its wording for the AI definition.

definition.text: Validity (with reliability) is a trustworthy-AI property: the degree to which an AI system measures or produces what it is intended to, confirmed by objective evidence.
definition.source: NIST AI 100-3 (The Language of Trustworthy AI)

terms.plain_language: Whether the system actually measures the thing you think it measures (not just consistently — that's reliability). A model can be reliably wrong.
terms.fss_note: Maps closely to FSS notions of accuracy/unbiasedness under SPD No. 2. NIST AI 100-3 is provisional (beta) — verify exact wording before formal reliance.

crosswalk.note: Both ask 'are we measuring the intended construct.' Largely compatible across the survey-methodology and AI senses; the survey literature splits it further (content/construct/criterion validity).

fss_context.text: Maps closely to FSS notions of accuracy/unbiasedness under SPD No. 2. NIST AI 100-3 is provisional (beta) — verify exact wording before formal reliance.

definition.text: The probability of performing a specified function without failure under given conditions for a specified period of time.

terms.plain_language: Whether the system gives the same answer under the same conditions — consistency. Reliable but invalid means consistently measuring the wrong thing.
terms.fss_note: In FSS quality terms, reliability maps to reproducibility across processing cycles. Note NIST uses 'reliability' in both a systems-engineering sense and a trustworthy-AI sense.

crosswalk.note: Compatible: both mean consistency of results under repetition.

fss_context.text: In FSS quality terms, reliability maps to reproducibility across processing cycles. Note NIST uses 'reliability' in both a systems-engineering sense and a trustworthy-AI sense.

definition.text: A short documentation artifact accompanying a trained model that reports its intended use, performance across conditions, evaluation data, limitations, and ethical considerations.
definition.source: Mitchell et al., 'Model Cards for Model Reporting' (FAT* 2019)

terms.plain_language: A standardized 'nutrition label' for an AI model: what it's for, how well it works, on whom it was tested, and where it fails. Agencies should demand one before adopting a model.
terms.fss_note: The AI analog of survey methodology/technical documentation; statistical agencies should require model cards (and 'system cards' for deployed systems) as an acquisition condition.

crosswalk.fss_concept: survey methodology documentation / technical documentation (SPD No. 2)
crosswalk.note: Both document methodology, performance, and appropriate-use conditions. Disanalogy: methodology docs describe a designed procedure; a model card reports empirical behavior of a learned model.

fss_context.text: Practitioner framing (NOT graph-grounded): the FSS should require a model card (and system card) as acquisition documentation for any adopted AI model — the AI analog of survey methodology documentation. The corpus references 'system cards' only in passing (M-24-10) and a 'GPT-4 System Card' citation; it neither defines nor mandates model cards, so this is a recommended practice, not a corpus obligation.

definition.text: In the context of computers and law enforcement use, it is an equivalent term to chain of custody. It involves the method of generation, transmission and storage of information that may be used to trace the origin of a piece of information processed by community resources.

terms.plain_language: The documented history of where data (or a model's training data) came from and how it was transformed — the chain of custody. Without it, you can't audit or reproduce a result.
terms.fss_note: In the FSS, provenance/metadata is required for data products (CIPSEA defines 'metadata' to include provenance, 44 U.S.C. § 3561(19)); for AI it extends to model and training-data lineage under data-governance requirements.

crosswalk.note: Provenance is the origin-and-transformation portion of the broader metadata an FSS product must carry.

fss_context.text: In the FSS, provenance/metadata is required for data products (CIPSEA defines 'metadata' to include provenance, 44 U.S.C. § 3561(19)); for AI it extends to model and training-data lineage under data-governance requirements.

definition.text: An AI system that requires human interaction.
definition.source: DoD Modeling & Simulation Glossary, via NIST AI 100-3 (AIRC glossary)

terms.plain_language: A design where a person reviews, approves, or can override the AI before its output takes effect — as opposed to fully automated action. 'On-the-loop' is lighter (monitoring) than 'in-the-loop' (approval). The point is meaningful human judgment, not a rubber stamp.
terms.fss_note: The control that keeps AI-assisted coding, imputation, or edit-flagging from acting unreviewed; the risk is automation bias — humans rubber-stamping AI when review volume exceeds what they can meaningfully audit. OMB M-25-21 and NIST AI 600-1 (Human-AI Configuration) are the references — pull exact wording.

crosswalk.fss_concept: analyst review / adjudication in survey processing
crosswalk.note: Both insert human judgment before an automated output is accepted. Disanalogy: automation bias makes the human review weaker than it looks as volume scales.

fss_context.text: The control that keeps AI-assisted coding, imputation, or edit-flagging from acting unreviewed; the risk is automation bias — humans rubber-stamping AI when review volume exceeds what they can meaningfully audit. OMB M-25-21 and NIST AI 600-1 (Human-AI Configuration) are the references — pull exact wording.

definition.text: Quality is defined as fitness for use: the degree to which data are suitable for the intended use(s), assessed across multiple quality dimensions (relevance, accuracy, timeliness, accessibility, interpretability, coherence).
definition.source: FCSM, A Framework for Data Quality (FCSM 20-04)

terms.plain_language: The principle that 'quality' isn't absolute — data are good enough only relative to a specific purpose. The same dataset can be fit for one use and unfit for another. This is the lens for judging whether an AI tool or its data is 'good enough' for a given task.
terms.fss_note: The FSS's governing definition of data quality (FCSM Data Quality Framework; this corpus holds FCSM 20-04 and 23-02). It is also the right frame for evaluating AI outputs: fit for which decision?

crosswalk.note: Identical principle the KG quality battery uses — quality is purpose-relative, so the user selects the dimensions that matter.

fss_context.text: The FSS's governing definition of data quality (FCSM Data Quality Framework; this corpus holds FCSM 20-04 and 23-02). It is also the right frame for evaluating AI outputs: fit for which decision?

definition.text: [prompts are] natural language requests that elicit accurate, high quality responses from a language model.
definition.source: Google Gemini API documentation, 'Prompt design strategies'

terms.plain_language: The text you type to an AI tool to tell it what you want; 'prompting' is the process of writing and providing such inputs to guide a generative model toward specific outputs. The same model gives very different answers to differently worded prompts — useful, and a reproducibility hazard.
terms.fss_note: No U.S.-federal definition of 'prompt' exists — federal docs describe prompts conceptually as natural-language user inputs to AI systems; this is a basic operational term, not policy vocabulary. FSS relevance: a prompt can inadvertently leak confidential text into a third-party model, and undocumented prompts make an AI-assisted result irreproducible — treat prompts as documented methodology.

fss_context.text: Practitioner framing (NOT graph-grounded): a prompt is the actual interface to a generative tool, so for an FSS AI-readiness review the concern is operational — an undocumented prompt makes an AI-assisted result irreproducible, and a prompt bearing confidential text can leak it to a third-party model. Not an FSS policy term; the corpus word-matches for 'prompt' are the English adjective, not this concept.

definition.text: Data is AI-ready when it is machine-understandable — not merely machine-readable: its context, provenance, methodology, and quality signals are preserved and programmatically queryable, sufficient for an AI system (particularly a large language model) to analyze and answer questions about the asset without loss of statistical integrity. Per the federal project definition, AI-readiness is “the extent to which a data asset is prepared for effective analysis and querying by AI systems, particularly LLMs,” spanning data quality, access, formatting, and metadata. Scope boundary: this definition assumes access to the data asset is already established — it addresses the readiness of data that a consuming system can already reach. (The discoverability/retrievability axis is treated separately in the forward-interpretation addendum, deliberately outside the grounded definition.)
definition.source: America’s DataHub RFS — Topic MLMU-25, “Measuring Large Language Model Understanding of Federal Statistical Data” (footnote 2); framed as an extension of FCSM 25-03 (AI-Ready Federal Statistical Data) and FCSM 20-04 (A Framework for Data Quality).

alt_definitions.text: For the purposes of this project, “AI-readiness” refers to the extent to which a data asset is prepared for effective analysis and querying by AI systems, particularly LLMs. This includes data quality, access, formatting, and metadata. AI-ready data enable LLMs to generate meaningful insights from both structured (e.g., tabular) and unstructured (e.g., reports) formats by facilitating accurate interpretation and contextual understanding.
alt_definitions.source: America’s DataHub RFS, Topic MLMU-25, footnote 2 (verbatim, verified against source 2026-06-23)

terms.plain_language: Data is “AI-ready” when a machine — typically an LLM — can not just open it but actually understand it: the numbers arrive with enough context, documentation, provenance, and quality information that the model interprets them correctly and answers questions without mangling the statistics. Machine-readable (a clean CSV) is necessary but not sufficient; machine-understandable is the bar. Note this assumes a system can already get to the data — whether it can find and retrieve it in the first place is a separate question (see the addendum).
terms.fss_note: The project’s flagship evaluation target: you cannot evaluate AI-data readiness without a definition, and none is ratified FSS-wide. It extends the FCSM data-quality framework (20-04) into the AI-consumption setting (25-03) — the same dimensions (accuracy, accessibility, timeliness) now judged by whether an LLM can consume the asset without losing statistical integrity. It builds on FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) rather than superseding it: FAIR’s Accessible and Interoperable are ancestors of the access and machine-understandability concerns here.

crosswalk.fss_concept: Lawrence Data Readiness Levels (2017), Bands C/B/A — academic conceptual ancestor (arXiv:1705.02245)
crosswalk.note: Lawrence’s Data Readiness Levels are the conceptual ancestor: Band C = accessibility (can the data be accessed), Band B = faithfulness/validity (is it what it claims to be), Band A = data-in-context (ready for a specific, defined task). AI-readiness inherits the use-case-conditional logic — Lawrence shows readiness is relative to the intended use (this use-case-conditionality point is Lawrence’s, 2017; it is NOT attributed to Gartner). Disanalogy: DRL is a general-purpose data-maturity scale; this definition is scoped to federal statistical data and adds the “without loss of statistical integrity” constraint a general maturity scale does not carry. Cited as an external ancestor only — academic, not federal policy, so not ingested into the corpus.

fss_context.text: Grounded in the corpus: the federal definition (America’s DataHub / NSDS RFS) plus the FCSM 25-03 AI-ready extension and the FCSM 20-04 data-quality framework. The corpus establishes that creating AI-ready data “builds on established open-data principles” and machine-readability (FCSM 25-03 §s34), that federal statistics used in AI interactions must meet the Framework’s data-quality dimensions — accessibility, timeliness, accuracy (§s30) — and that metadata enrichment exposed through AI-friendly APIs improves how LLMs discover and interpret data (§s31). FCSM 20-04 §s184 anchors the data-quality dimensions that must be balanced and preserved.

forward_interpretation.label: Forward interpretation — ahead of documented policy (NOT an established standard)
forward_interpretation.text: Practitioner framing (NOT graph-grounded; ahead of the documented standard): the federal and Commerce/FCSM definitions above assume access to the data asset is already established. In practice, access and machine-retrievability must be deliberately configured — via emerging mechanisms such as llms.txt, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and WebMCP, the last standardized only around 2026, i.e. AFTER the FCSM/Commerce literature was authored. Written policy lags the technology in this fast-moving field. A complete AI-data-readiness picture therefore treats discoverability and retrievability as a PRIOR axis the current standards do not yet cover: data a model cannot find or reach is not AI-ready in any operational sense, however machine-understandable it is once reached. This is the machine-as-first-class-user position, offered as a forward-looking, bounded thought-leadership seed — not a mandate, and deliberately separated from the grounded definition so the definition’s authority is not diluted by interpretation.

flags.scope_boundary: Definition (Part A) covers the readiness of data a consuming system can already REACH; it assumes access is established. The discoverability/retrievability (access) axis is addressed only in the forward-interpretation addendum (Part B), explicitly ahead of policy.
flags.not_superseding: FAIR — AI-readiness builds on / extends FAIR; it does not supersede it.
